preamble: The fortition and devoicing of a palatal glide [j] to a voiceless palatal stop [c] is a process of consonantalization. This shift strengthens the glide into a stop while losing its voicing, often occurring in initial positions or under emphatic stress.

examples.note: Strengthening of palatal glides into voiceless stops in specific historical contexts.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
